title: Parātrīśikā Vivaraṇa: The Absolute (Anuttara) and Its Realisation
subtitle: Bettina Bäumer's study of Abhinavagupta's commentary on the Parātrīśikā Tantra — Anuttara, mantra mysticism, and the totality of reality

summary.one_line: Abhinavagupta's Parātrīśikā Vivaraṇa expounds the Absolute (Anuttara) as the ground of all reality, accessible through mantra, where 'everything is related to the totality' and 'every part contains...
summary.scope: Covers the concept of Anuttara as the unsurpassable absolute, the theory of mantra as the self-expression of the absolute, the phonemic emanation (varṇa-sṛṣṭi), the heart-seed mantra SAUḤ (hṛdayabīja), the relationship between the absolute and manifestation (from Anuttara to Kaulikīsṛṣṭi), and Abhinavagupta's method of integrating ritual, philosophy, and linguistic mysticism.

sources.label: Bäumer, Bettina. Parātrīśikā Vivaraṇa: The Absolute (Anuttara) and Its Realisation. A study and interpretation of Abhinavagupta's commentary on the Parātrīśikā Tantra.
Abhinavagupta, Parātrīśikā Vivaraṇa (PTV) — primary commentary on the Parātrīśikā Tantra
Parātrīśikā Tantra — the root text (31 verses) on which Abhinavagupta's PTV comments, focusing on the supreme goddess Parā and the mantra SAUḤ

body.text: Anuttara — the Unsurpassable, the Absolute — is the central concept of the Parātrīśikā Tantra and its commentary. The very first word of the Devī's question in the Tantra is 'Anuttara,' and Abhinavagupta's entire commentary is structured around unpacking its meanings. Anuttara is not merely a philosophical concept but the actual reality that is to be realised. It is called 'unsurpassable' because nothing surpasses it, nothing is outside it, and it is the ground of all that is. Bäumer identifies several layers of meaning: Anuttara as the bestower of the perfection of totality (kaulikasiddhi), Anuttara as the interconnectedness of all things (sarvam sarvātmakam — everything is related to the totality), and Anuttara as the source from which manifestation unfolds (from Anuttara to Kaulikīsṛṣṭi).

The central mantra of the Parātrīśikā is SAUḤ, the 'seed of the heart' (hṛdayabīja). Abhinavagupta devotes substantial portions of his commentary to this single syllable, analysing its phonemic components (S + AU + Ḥ) and their cosmic significance. SAUḤ is not merely a sound that represents the goddess Parā — it IS Parā in sonic form. The mantra contains within itself the entire process of creation (sṛṣṭibīja) and the power of the absolute to manifest as the universe. The practice of this mantra includes its mental repetition, ritual offering, and the realisation of its identity with one's own consciousness. Bäumer notes that the mantra provides a direct access to Anuttara because it is itself a manifestation of Anuttara as sound.

The phonemic emanation (varṇa-sṛṣṭi) is the process by which the absolute articulates itself as the universe through the fifty phonemes of the Sanskrit alphabet. In verses 9cd-11 of the Parātrīśikā, the central mantra SAUḤ is revealed, with each phoneme corresponding to a power of the absolute. The phonemes are not arbitrary signs but the actual building blocks of reality — consciousness articulating itself as sound, sound condensing into form. Bäumer explains that this is not a theory of creation ex nihilo but of manifestation: the absolute does not create something outside itself but expresses itself through its own vibrational nature (spanda). The fifty phonemes are the fifty forms of Śiva's reflective awareness (vimarśa).

The structure of the Parātrīśikā Vivaraṇa follows the movement from the Absolute to manifestation: Anuttara → the heart-seed mantra SAUḤ → the powers of the mantra → the fruit attained by its practice → the ritual practice (vidhi). This is not a linear fall but a conscious self-expression of the absolute. Bäumer emphasises that in Abhinavagupta's system, ritual is never mere mechanical action — it is the external form of an internal realisation. The mantra practices described in the PTV are designed to lead the practitioner back through the stages of manifestation to the source, Anuttara. This is the essence of the Kaula path: realising the totality (kaulikasiddhi) through the recognition that every part contains the whole.

gaps: Full text is 18,920 lines — extracted key theses on Anuttara, SAUḤ mantra, phonemic emanation, and ritual structure. Detailed ritual procedures not extracted.

versions.changes: Initial extraction from Bäumer's study. Full text at /tmp/tantraloka4.txt
